text: This video demonstrates the combined procedure of laparoscopic and intraspector resection for low rectal cancer. This procedure is pioneered by Eric Rollier, professor of surgery in Bordeaux. He divided the low rectal cancer into four types depending on the distance from the dentate line. This young lady has a type two anterior tumour. shown here on the MRI to be two centimeters from the dentate line. The tumor was downstaged by long course chemoradiotherapy.

The surgeon uses five laparoscopic ports to access the abdominal cavity. Dissection begins at the inferior mesenteric vein, just distal to the DJ flexure, and proceeds laterally to expose the ureter towards the spleen and splenic flexure. The dissection continues at the level of the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring the ureter is well exposed and protected.
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery, securing it with Hem-o-lok clips to control blood flow.
The surgeon controls and divides the mesenteric vein. The sigmoid colon is then mobilized from lateral to medial using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.
The surgeon utilizes an energy device with a monopolar tip and bipolar ligature technology, likely for precise tissue cutting and coagulation during the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon continues the lateral to medial dissection, mobilizing the splenic flexure, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum within the total mesorectal excision (TME) planes, using adequate traction and counter-traction through the left iliac fossa and suprapubic ports.
The surgeon divides the peritoneal reflection anterior to the rectum and continues the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection down towards the pelvic floor.

The laparoscopic dissection progresses into the intersphincteric plane, dividing the rectococcygeal fibers from the abdominal side. A Lone Star retractor is used to facilitate transanal dissection. The rectum is divided above the dentate line and resected in the intersphincteric plane, connecting with the abdominal approach.
The surgeon completes the dissection and extracts the rectum transanally. The specimen is then divided at the proximal resection margin.

The surgeon prepares the colon for anastomosis, ensuring there is no tension and that the blood supply to the edge is adequate. The colon is sutured to the dentate line using a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis technique. The colon is then rechecked for proper tension and orientation.

A tube drain is inserted through the left iliac fossa port and positioned in the pelvis. Subsequently, a defunctioning loop ileostomy is created in the right iliac fossa, and the surgical wounds are closed.
